title: What Can You Do With a Degree in the Humanities? (More Than You Think)
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You tell someone you are majoring in English, history, philosophy, or a foreign language, and they ask the question you have learned to dread: what are you going to do with that? The question implies that the answer is nothing. It implies that you have chosen a degree with no career prospects, that you will be overeducated and underemployed, and that you should have majored in something practical. The implication is wrong. Humanities graduates are not doomed to unemployment. They are spread across nearly every industry, often in roles that did not exist when they declared their major. Here is what the data actually says and how to build a career with a humanities degree.

The Data Does Not Support the Panic

The narrative that humanities majors are unemployable is not supported by evidence. Humanities graduates do have slightly higher unemployment rates immediately after graduation compared to some professional majors, but the gap narrows significantly over time. By mid-career, humanities graduates have employment rates comparable to graduates in many professional fields. The early gap is real, but it is temporary.

The earnings picture is more complex. Humanities majors do earn less on average than engineering or computer science majors. But the average conceals enormous variation. A history major who becomes a lawyer, a philosophy major who becomes a software engineer, or an English major who becomes a technical writer earns a salary comparable to or exceeding many STEM graduates. The major is not destiny. The career path is.

What humanities graduates lack in specialized technical training, they often gain in skills that are valued across industries. The ability to analyze complex texts, construct persuasive arguments, communicate clearly in writing, and understand cultural and historical context are not soft skills. They are foundational skills that become more valuable as careers progress and technical skills become outdated. The entry-level job market may favor specific technical skills. The promotion market favors the ability to think, write, and communicate.

Where Humanities Graduates Actually Work

Law is one of the most common destinations. History, philosophy, and English are among the most common undergraduate majors for law school applicants. The skills developed in these majors, close reading, argumentation, and writing, are directly applicable to legal reasoning. Law schools do not prefer specific majors. They prefer students who can read carefully and write persuasively.

Business and consulting hire humanities graduates in large numbers. Management consulting firms recruit from all majors, not just business and economics. The case interview, the core of the consulting recruiting process, tests analytical thinking and communication, not specific technical knowledge. A philosophy major who can structure an argument and communicate it clearly is well-prepared for this format.

Technology companies employ humanities graduates in roles that require understanding users, communicating clearly, and thinking about ethical implications. User experience research, content design, technical writing, product management, and policy roles often prefer or require the skills humanities majors develop. A computer can write code. It cannot understand why users behave the way they do or what a piece of writing should say to achieve its purpose.

Media, publishing, and journalism are traditional destinations, but they are not the only ones. Humanities graduates work in marketing, advertising, public relations, and corporate communications. They work in government, policy analysis, and diplomacy. They work in education, both in classrooms and in educational technology and administration. They work in nonprofit organizations, museums, and cultural institutions. The list is long because the skills are broadly applicable.

How to Build Employable Skills Alongside Your Humanities Degree

The degree alone is not a job ticket. No degree is. What matters is what you do alongside your coursework. Pursue internships in fields that interest you, even if they are not obviously connected to your major. A history major who interns at a marketing agency is building a bridge between their academic skills and a career path. The internship demonstrates that you can apply your skills in a professional context.

Develop quantitative literacy. You do not need to major in math, but you should be comfortable with data. Take a statistics course. Learn to use spreadsheets competently. Understand how to read and interpret quantitative information. The humanities graduate who can analyze a text and a dataset is far more valuable than the one who can only do the first.

Build a portfolio of work. Your essays and research papers demonstrate your analytical and writing skills, but they are written for an academic audience. Create writing samples that are aimed at a broader audience. A blog, a newsletter, or contributions to a campus publication. The portfolio shows employers what you can produce, not just what you studied.

Learn to talk about your skills in professional language. Close reading becomes analytical thinking. Writing essays becomes communicating complex ideas clearly. Constructing arguments becomes persuasive communication. The skills are the same. The framing makes them legible to employers who do not understand what a humanities degree involves.

Network intentionally. Your professors, alumni from your department, and guest speakers in your courses are connections to careers. Ask them how they got from the major to their current role. The answers will reveal paths you did not know existed. Most humanities graduates did not follow a straight line from degree to career. They made moves, tried things, and adjusted. The stories of how they navigated are more useful than any career guide.

The Bottom Line

Humanities degrees do not lead to unemployment. They lead to a wide range of careers, many of which are not obvious from the name of the major. The skills developed in humanities coursework, analysis, argumentation, writing, and cultural understanding, are broadly valued and become more valuable over time. Build employable skills alongside your degree through internships, quantitative literacy, a portfolio, and networking. Learn to describe your skills in professional language. The question is not what can you do with a humanities degree. It is what will you do with yours.
